rationale_for_baseline_tier: A frail 85-year-old in decompensated hypovolaemic shock — hypotension (clinician BP 85/50), tachycardia (HR 115), NEW confusion and 12 hours of anuria (pre-renal AKI) after 24 hours of intractable vomiting and diarrhoea, with the oral route completely failed. The definitive treatment is intravenous rehydration, which telehealth cannot deliver. She is bedbound, confused and hypotensive and cannot safely self-transport, so an emergency ambulance (000) to the Emergency Department is the correct tier. The administrative 'IV therapy' title must not lower the tier: this is a genuine emergency with patient/caregiver-reportable time-critical features (confusion, no urine, collapse on standing). Under-triaging it is the 3x-weighted error.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening confusion or drowsiness, or collapse/near-collapse on any attempt to sit or stand
escalation_edges.rationale: Deepening delirium or collapse indicates progressing cerebral hypoperfusion/decompensated shock; emergency ambulance and resuscitation-capable care are required now.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes more confused or drowsy, or collapses when moved, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and keep her lying flat.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Systolic blood pressure remains below 90 despite fluids, or she remains completely anuric
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If SBP <90 persists, or no urine for a further 6 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent hypotension or ongoing anuria signals refractory shock and established/worsening AKI — emergency transfer and inpatient care are mandatory.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If her blood pressure stays very low despite fluids, or she still hasn't passed any urine after another 6 hours, transfer her to the Emergency Department by ambulance (000) without delay.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Inability to give any fluid at all / ongoing vomiting with continued deterioration
escalation_edges.rationale: A completely failed oral route with continued decline needs emergency-department parenteral rehydration; it cannot be managed remotely or deferred to routine hours.
escalation_edges.communication_template: She cannot get better on sips alone while she keeps vomiting — she needs to be seen in the Emergency Department today for fluids given into a vein.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New severe, localised abdominal pain, a rigid/distended abdomen, or blood in the vomit or stool
escalation_edges.rationale: These features point away from simple gastroenteritis toward a surgical abdomen (obstruction/ischaemia/perforation) or GI haemorrhage and mandate emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she develops severe pain in one part of her tummy, a hard swollen abdomen, or any blood in her vomit or bowel motions, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New breathlessness or chest tightness (possible fluid overload) once any drip is running
escalation_edges.rationale: A frail elder can be tipped into pulmonary oedema by rapid IV fluids; new breathlessness during infusion is an emergency requiring the treating team/000.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes short of breath or her chest feels tight while a drip is running, alert staff and call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lowers the threshold for emergency escalation — shock decompensates faster and fluid resuscitation must be monitored for overload; do not attempt watchful waiting or home management.
Perindopril must remain withheld during hypovolaemia/AKI to avoid worsening hypotension and renal injury; flag reintroduction only after recovery.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Resident in a residential aged care facility with nursing staff present
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Nursing staff can provide observations, maintain a fluid-balance chart, position her safely and arrange/accompany emergency transfer — but on-site nursing does not substitute for the IV rehydration and monitoring that requires ED-level care here.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is an emergency. Call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them she is a frail elderly resident with severe dehydration, low blood pressure, confusion and no urine output who cannot keep fluids down and needs urgent fluids into a vein. Keep her lying flat; do not let her sit up quickly, stand or walk. Keep her blood-pressure tablet (perindopril) withheld. Have the nursing staff stay with her, keep a record of any vomiting and urine, and unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance immediately
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this must not wait for in-hours or the facility GP's next visit.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Send the fluid-balance chart, the observation/vital-sign record and the current medication list (noting perindopril withheld) with the resident; the facility GP should review renal function the next morning and decide on reintroducing regular medications.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the encounter as a routine same-day GP visit, an infusion-clinic booking, or home oral rehydration — anchored by the 'IV therapy' title — despite shock, confusion and anuria.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delay in parenteral rehydration allows progression to established acute tubular necrosis, a catastrophic fall, or hypovolaemic collapse overnight in a frail elder.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Correctly identifying an emergency and directing the patient to the Emergency Department, but by self/facility transport rather than an ambulance for a bedbound, confused, hypotensive patient — risking deterioration or collapse during unmonitored transfer without resuscitation-capable escort.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct baseline here, so it is not over-triage; over-triage would only apply if a comparable but milder dehydration (well-perfused, alert, passing urine, tolerating sips) were escalated to 000 rather than urgent GP review.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure the blood pressure, confirm the degree of AKI, or fully exclude occult sepsis or an early surgical abdomen.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the reported red-flag constellation (confusion, anuria, postural collapse, failed oral route) as decompensated shock and escalate to emergency in-person care rather than seeking remote confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Waiting for the facility GP's next visit, ordering an outpatient blood test, or trialling home oral fluids to 'see how she goes' before escalating.
